title: GET is safe and idempotent — body must not have side effects
content:
GET retrieves a representation. Per RFC 9110 §9.3.1, GET is both safe (no observable state change beyond logging/metering) and idempotent (N identical GETs equal one). Caches, retry middleware, and prefetchers rely on this contract.

Why: when GET is safe, every layer above the handler — browsers, proxies, retry libraries, search-engine crawlers — can speculate on it. Break the contract once and you get duplicated charges, inflated counters, and unrepeatable bug reports.

When: every read endpoint. If the action mutates state, the verb is POST or PATCH, not GET — even if the URL reads like a query.

title: POST is non-idempotent — design retries around Idempotency-Key
content:
POST is neither safe nor idempotent (RFC 9110 §9.3.3). A retried POST may create two of the same resource. The well-known fix is the Idempotency-Key header (draft-ietf-httpapi-idempotency-key, Stripe convention): client generates a UUID per logical action, server stores the request hash + response for ≥24h.

Why: networks drop responses, not requests. The client has no way to know whether the previous POST landed. Without an idempotency key, retries either double-charge or never retry.

When: every state-creating POST that a client might retry — payments, sign-ups, sends. Read-only POSTs (search by big body) skip the key.

title: PUT replaces the resource at a known URI
content:
PUT writes a complete representation to the URI in the request line (RFC 9110 §9.3.4). It is idempotent: PUT /users/42 with the same body yields the same state. The server may create the resource if it doesn't exist.

Why: idempotent replacement is the simplest mental model for state sync. Clients that crash mid-request can replay safely; reconciliation loops can converge.

When: the client knows the resource URI in advance (own-id schemes, content-addressed paths). For server-assigned ids, POST to a collection.

title: PATCH applies a partial update — never required to be idempotent
content:
PATCH carries a delta to apply (RFC 5789 + RFC 9110 §9.3.5). Whether a particular PATCH is idempotent depends on the patch document. JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7396) is idempotent for absolute fields. JSON Patch (RFC 6902) op `add` to an array is not.

Why: clients and servers must agree on the patch format up front. A client that assumes idempotency on a non-idempotent PATCH will double-apply on retry.

When: partial updates where sending the full PUT body is wasteful or racy. State the patch media type in `Content-Type` (`application/merge-patch+json` or `application/json-patch+json`).

title: DELETE is idempotent — second delete returns 404 or 204
content:
DELETE removes the resource (RFC 9110 §9.3.5). Idempotent: DELETE /users/42 twice leaves the system in the same state as once. The first call returns 204 (or 200 with body). The second returns 404 — the resource is genuinely gone.

Why: retry-safe deletion lets clients implement at-least-once semantics. The 404-on-repeat is the contract; clients treat it as success-of-the-second-kind.

When: any destructive endpoint. For soft-deletes (set deleted_at), still return 204 on the first call so the client can move on.

title: HEAD returns headers only — used for existence + cache probes
content:
HEAD is GET without the body (RFC 9110 §9.3.2). The server must compute the same headers (Content-Length, ETag, Last-Modified) it would on GET so caches can revalidate cheaply.

Why: HEAD lets a client check `is this resource still 200, has its ETag changed, how big is the body` without paying the body bytes. Useful for big binary downloads and cache-warmth probes.

When: support HEAD on every resource endpoint that supports GET. Most frameworks do this for free; verify the headers actually match.

title: OPTIONS reports allowed methods plus CORS preflight
content:
OPTIONS describes the communication options for the target (RFC 9110 §9.3.7). Two roles: respond to CORS preflight with the configured Access-Control-* headers, and report the `Allow` header listing the methods the resource accepts.

Why: clients (especially browsers) need a way to discover what's permitted before sending a non-simple request. Without OPTIONS, every preflight is a guess.

When: every endpoint exposed to browsers. Frameworks usually wire this up; verify the Allow header on each route includes only the methods you actually implement.

title: 201 Created with Location header for successful POST-creates
content:
When a POST creates a resource, return 201 with a `Location` header pointing at the new URI (RFC 9110 §15.3.2). Body is the new representation; clients parse Location to track the id without parsing the body.

Why: separating the side-channel (Location) from the body lets clients wire up redirects, navigation, and cache pre-warming on a stable header. Body shapes change; the header contract doesn't.

When: every create endpoint. If creation is async, see 202 Accepted with a status URL instead.

title: 202 Accepted for async work — return a status URL
content:
202 means the request is valid and queued, but not yet executed (RFC 9110 §15.3.3). Body or Location header points at a status resource the client polls (or subscribes to via SSE/WebSocket).

Why: long jobs (video transcode, report build) blow past HTTP timeouts. 202 with a status URL decouples acceptance from completion and gives the client a stable handle to check progress.

When: any operation that might exceed ~5s. The status resource ideally returns 200 while in-flight (with progress fields) and 303 See Other to the result when done.

title: 204 No Content for successful DELETE and empty PUT
content:
204 says the request succeeded and there is no body to send (RFC 9110 §15.3.5). Browsers and clients must not change the displayed view. The response MUST NOT include a message body — content-length zero or absent.

Why: the empty-body contract lets clients short-circuit body parsing and lets caches store the negative-body result. A non-empty body on 204 confuses both.

When: idempotent endpoints where the result is well known (DELETE, PUT-of-known-state, settings save). If you have anything to say, use 200.

title: 207 Multi-Status only for batch endpoints documented as such
content:
207 (RFC 4918, used outside WebDAV by some batch APIs) returns per-sub-request statuses inside one outer 200/207 envelope. Each entry has its own status code and body.

Why: bulk endpoints either succeed-or-fail atomically (one outer status), or partial-succeed with per-item status (207). 207 makes the per-item shape explicit and avoids the ambiguous `200 with errors[]` pattern.

When: bulk-mutation endpoints where partial success is the right semantics. Document the inner-row schema and link to it in the outer body. Plain CRUD APIs do not need 207.

title: Returning 200 OK with an error envelope in the body
content:
Trigger: framework or team convention that every response is `{ "ok": false, "error": "..." }` with HTTP status 200, regardless of what happened.

Consequence: HTTP middleware, retry libraries, browser cache rules, monitoring dashboards, and CDN edge logic all read the status code, not the body. A real failure painted as 200 is invisible to every layer above the handler. Every client invents its own envelope parser; cross-team integrations break silently.

Alternative: use the right status code (4xx for client error, 5xx for server error) and an `application/problem+json` body (RFC 9457). Status code is the wire-level signal; problem details fill in the human + machine context.

title: PUT used to create with a server-assigned id
content:
Trigger: client POSTs to /orders, then 'reuses' PUT /orders/<id> on retry, expecting the server to allocate the id.

Consequence: PUT is replacement at a known URI (RFC 9110 §9.3.4). PUT to a URI the server is supposed to invent breaks the idempotency contract — the server either reuses the wrong id, allocates a new one each retry, or both. Caches and proxies see PUT and assume the URI is stable. It isn't.

Alternative: POST to the collection URL for server-assigned ids; PUT only for client-known-id schemes. If the client wants idempotency on POST, use the Idempotency-Key header.

title: Using 404 to hide authentication failures from anonymous callers
content:
Trigger: 'security through obscurity' — return 404 instead of 401 to avoid confirming the resource exists.

Consequence: the trick only works for one anonymous probe. Authenticated callers get 200 for resources they have access to and 403 for ones they don't, so the 404-vs-403 boundary still leaks the existence map to anyone with a valid token. Meanwhile, every legitimate client now retries 404s, mis-attributes auth failures to missing routes, and breaks observability dashboards that count 4xx by intent.

Alternative: return 401 for missing/invalid auth, 403 for valid auth without permission, 404 only for genuinely missing resources. If existence-leakage is a real threat (e.g. private repos), return 404 uniformly to BOTH authenticated and anonymous callers — same shape, no oracle.

title: 401 means missing or invalid authentication, 403 means authorization denied
content:
401 (Unauthorized — misnamed; should be 'Unauthenticated') signals the request lacks valid credentials. 403 (Forbidden) signals credentials are valid but not allowed for this action (RFC 9110 §15.5.2 / §15.5.4). 401 MUST carry a `WWW-Authenticate` challenge.

Why: the two map to different client behaviours. 401 → redirect to login or refresh token. 403 → show a permission-denied UI. Conflating them sends users into login loops on resources they will never reach.

When: every authed endpoint. The decision tree: no token / bad token → 401. Good token, wrong scope/role → 403.

title: 409 Conflict for state-machine clashes, not for validation errors
content:
409 means the request conflicts with the current state of the target (RFC 9110 §15.5.10). Examples: trying to delete an order that's already shipped, creating a username that's taken, committing on a stale version.

Why: 409 tells the client the request shape is fine but the world isn't. Retrying without changes will fail again — the client must re-fetch and re-decide. 422 says 'your input is invalid'; 412 says 'your precondition didn't match'; 409 says 'state moved underneath you'.

When: optimistic concurrency clashes (returns 412 if If-Match was used; 409 if not), uniqueness violations, illegal state transitions.

title: 412 Precondition Failed signals optimistic-concurrency clash
content:
When a request carries `If-Match: "<etag>"` or `If-Unmodified-Since: <date>` and the precondition fails, return 412 (RFC 9110 §15.5.13). The client knows its view of the resource is stale and must re-fetch.

Why: 412 is the precise wire signal for 'someone else updated this between your GET and your PUT'. 409 is broader; 412 is the contract for conditional requests.

When: every PUT/PATCH/DELETE endpoint where lost updates would be a bug. The client GETs, reads ETag, sends back If-Match on the next mutation. Server returns 412 if the resource has moved.

title: 422 Unprocessable Content for semantic validation failures
content:
422 says the request was syntactically valid (parses, headers fine) but semantically wrong (RFC 9110 §15.5.21): missing required field, value out of range, referenced entity doesn't exist. 400 is for malformed requests (broken JSON, wrong content-type).

Why: separating syntax from semantics lets clients route errors. 400 → bug in the client's serialiser. 422 → bug in the user's input. Lumping both into 400 collapses the signal.

When: every validating endpoint. Pair with `application/problem+json` and a `validation_errors` extension array enumerating per-field issues.

title: 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header
content:
429 signals rate-limit hit (RFC 6585, kept by RFC 9110). Always include `Retry-After` (seconds, or HTTP-date) so well-behaved clients back off without guessing. Pair with the draft RateLimit-* headers (Limit / Remaining / Reset) for proactive throttling.

Why: clients without Retry-After either spin (DOS-ing your service) or apply random jitter that's worse than your bucket schedule. The header is the contract.

When: every rate-limited endpoint. Different from 503 — 429 means 'this client, this window'; 503 means 'system overloaded'.

title: 503 Service Unavailable with Retry-After for planned downtime
content:
503 signals the server is temporarily unavailable (RFC 9110 §15.6.4). Add `Retry-After: <seconds>` for planned maintenance, omit for unplanned outage so clients use their own back-off.

Why: 503 lets load balancers, browsers, and orchestrators distinguish 'try again in 30s' from a real 5xx fault. Health probes look for it explicitly.

When: maintenance windows, queue-full conditions, deliberate brownouts. Don't reuse 503 for rate limiting (use 429) — different semantics for clients.

title: 301 vs 308 — choose by whether the method may change
content:
301 Moved Permanently allows clients to switch POST → GET on follow (legacy browser behaviour). 308 Permanent Redirect (RFC 7538, RFC 9110 §15.4.9) preserves the method and body. 302 / 307 are the temporary equivalents.

Why: APIs almost always want method preservation. Returning 301 to a POST and watching the client retry as GET is a classic data-loss bug. 308 is unambiguous.

When: API redirects: always 308 (permanent) or 307 (temporary). 301/302 are for browser navigation where method-rewrite is acceptable.

title: 405 Method Not Allowed must include Allow header
content:
When a route exists but rejects the verb used, return 405 with an `Allow` header listing the methods it does accept (RFC 9110 §15.5.6). Returning 405 without `Allow` is a protocol violation.

Why: the Allow header is the only way for the client to discover the right verb without trial-and-error. Frameworks often skip it, leaving CORS preflights and method-discovering tools blind.

When: every 405 response. Auto-derive Allow from the route's registered handlers; do not hand-maintain.

title: Status code allowlist over the full HTTP code set
content:
Chose a documented 12-code allowlist (200, 201, 202, 204, 301, 304, 308, 400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 422, 429, 500, 503). Alternatives considered: use any code from the registry; just 200/4xx/5xx.

Reason: a small allowlist forces consistent decisions across teams. New developers don't reach for 226 IM Used or 451 Unavailable For Legal Reasons unless the case actually fits. Clients have a narrower table to switch on. Every code in the allowlist has a written 'use this when' rule in the docs.

Reversible: yes; widening the list is additive and non-breaking. Narrowing later means renaming codes in deployed clients, which is breaking.

title: application/problem+json for every error response body
content:
Every error response carries `Content-Type: application/problem+json` and a body matching RFC 9457 (or its predecessor RFC 7807). Required fields: `type` (URI), `title`, `status`. Optional: `detail`, `instance`. Domain extensions go in custom keys.

Why: a single, stable error envelope means every client reads errors the same way. The `type` URI is the durable identifier — clients dispatch on it without parsing English. New error types are additive.

When: every 4xx and 5xx response that has a body. 204 / 304 stay empty; everything else carries problem+json.

title: Problem-details type URI is the stable identifier — match on it
content:
Per RFC 9457 §3.1, `type` is a URI that identifies the problem class. Clients SHOULD dispatch on the URI, not on `title` or `detail` (which are human-readable and may change per locale or per release).

Why: stable types let clients localise titles, group errors, and write conditional retry logic. Match-on-detail couples the client to copy that may change next release.

When: every problem-details body. Use a URN like `urn:problem-type:account/insufficient-funds` or an absolute URL into your docs site (`https://api.acme.com/errors/insufficient-funds`). Do not reuse a URI across breaking versions.

title: Problem-details extensions for domain-specific error context
content:
RFC 9457 §3.2 allows extension members on the problem object: any keys the type's documentation defines. Common: `validation_errors` (array of {field, message}), `retry_after`, `quota_remaining`.

Why: extensions keep the core envelope stable while letting each error type carry the data clients actually need to recover. Sticking everything into `detail` defeats machine consumption.

When: any error class that needs structured context. Document the extension shape next to the type URI; treat it as part of the contract.

title: Variable data in problem-details title field
content:
Trigger: code interpolates the user's input into `title` — `"Email tom@example.com is already taken"`.

Consequence: per RFC 9457 §3.1, `title` is human-readable and should be stable per type. Variable titles break i18n (you can't translate user-substituted strings cleanly), break alerting rules that group by title, and break any client that uses title as a fallback discriminator. Sensitive values may also leak into log aggregators that index titles.

Alternative: `title` stays constant per type (`"Email already taken"`). Variable data lives in `detail` (free-form prose) or an extension (`{"email": "tom@example.com"}`).

title: Problem-details status field mirrors the HTTP status code
content:
RFC 9457 §3.1.1: when present, the `status` member SHOULD equal the HTTP response status code. Some intermediaries strip status codes; an in-body copy is the lifeline.

Why: redundancy is the contract. Logging pipelines, CDN edge inspectors, and clients behind misbehaving proxies can recover the original status from the body when the wire status is lost.

When: every problem-details body — set status to the same number you put in the HTTP status line. Drift between the two is a bug.

title: Problem-details instance URI for per-occurrence references
content:
`instance` is a URI for the specific occurrence of the problem (RFC 9457 §3.1). Useful targets: a request id (`/errors/req_abc123`), the failing resource (`/orders/42`), or a support-ticket-able identifier.

Why: support engineers can look up one specific failure without grepping the whole log corpus. The URI is opaque to the client but resolvable on the server side.

When: any error worth investigating after the fact. For trivial validation errors, instance is optional; for 5xx and authz denials, always include it.

title: RFC 9457 over RFC 7807 for greenfield problem-details
content:
Chose RFC 9457. Alternatives considered: RFC 7807 (the original), custom envelope.

Reason: RFC 9457 (July 2023) obsoletes RFC 7807 with stricter rules around extension members and clearer i18n guidance, while staying wire-compatible — a 9457 body parses fine as a 7807 body. New code should target the stricter spec; legacy 7807 deployments don't need to migrate.

Reversible: trivially. The extra rules in 9457 are tightening, not new fields. A 9457-shaped response is a valid 7807 response.

title: Stable error-code taxonomy versioned alongside the API
content:
Maintain a written catalogue of every problem-type URI: code, title, when it's raised, what extensions accompany it. Treat the catalogue as part of the API contract — additive changes only inside a major version.

Why: clients write switch statements on type URIs. Renaming or removing a type is a breaking change exactly like deleting a field. A catalogue makes the contract auditable.

When: any API that ships error responses. The catalogue lives in the docs alongside the OpenAPI spec; changes go through the same review process as endpoint changes.

title: ETag with If-None-Match for conditional GET caching
content:
Server returns `ETag: "<token>"` on every cacheable response (RFC 9110 §13.1.3). Client repeats with `If-None-Match: "<token>"`; server returns 304 Not Modified (empty body) when unchanged.

Why: 304 saves the body bytes — typically the bulk of the response — while still confirming freshness. CDNs, browsers, and HTTP clients implement this for free if the server emits the headers.

When: every GET on a representation that changes occasionally. Pair with `Cache-Control: max-age=N` to let clients skip the conditional check entirely for a short window.

title: ETag with If-Match for optimistic concurrency on PUT and PATCH
content:
Client sends `If-Match: "<etag>"` with the mutation. Server checks against the current ETag and returns 412 Precondition Failed if they differ (RFC 9110 §13.1.1). On match, the write proceeds and the response carries a new ETag.

Why: conditional writes are the cleanest defence against lost updates. Clients learn about the clash before committing further changes; servers don't need a lock.

When: every mutation endpoint where two clients might race. The cost is one round-trip and one extra header — far less than reconciling silently overwritten state.

title: Last-Modified plus If-Modified-Since as a lower-precision fallback
content:
When generating a stable ETag is expensive, fall back on `Last-Modified: <HTTP-date>` plus `If-Modified-Since` (RFC 9110 §13.1.4). Resolution is one second, so two writes within the same second look identical to the client.

Why: cheap to compute (the stat() time on the underlying record), good enough for many cache-validation cases, and required by some legacy proxies that don't honour ETag.

When: large blob endpoints where computing a content hash on every read is too expensive. For everything else, ETag is the better choice.

title: Cache-Control directives — choose by who may store the response
content:
`no-store` forbids any cache from storing the response. `no-cache` allows storage but mandates revalidation before serving. `private` allows browser caching but forbids shared caches (CDNs). `public` plus `max-age=N` is the standard shared-cache directive (RFC 9111 §5.2).

Why: each directive answers a different question. Conflating them — `no-cache, no-store, private` everywhere — disables CDNs needlessly and slows the API.

When: derive from the response: contains user-specific data → `private`; contains secrets → `no-store`; static representation → `public, max-age=...`.

title: Vary header lists every request header that varies the response
content:
`Vary: Accept-Encoding, Accept-Language, Authorization` tells caches the response is not fungible across these headers (RFC 9110 §12.5.5). Without Vary, a CDN may serve a French response to an English caller, or a logged-in body to anonymous viewers.

Why: shared caches use Vary as the cache-key extension. Forgetting one header is a data-leak class bug — the most common is omitting `Authorization` and serving cached private responses.

When: every response that varies by request header. List the union, not the intersection — too many Varys hurt cache hit rate but can't leak; too few can.

title: Omitting Vary: Authorization on auth-scoped responses
content:
Trigger: an authed endpoint returns user-specific data with `Cache-Control: private, max-age=60`, but no `Vary: Authorization` header.

Consequence: any intermediate cache (corporate proxy, ISP transparent cache, a misconfigured shared CDN) keys on URL alone. Tom's request fills the cache; Susan's request hits it and reads Tom's data. Class: data leak via cache poisoning.

Alternative: always include `Vary: Authorization` (and any other headers the response varies on) on responses that depend on the caller's identity. Pair with `Cache-Control: private` so well-behaved shared caches refuse to store at all.

title: Strong vs weak ETags — choose by byte-equality semantics
content:
Strong ETag (`"abc123"`) means byte-for-byte identical responses share the value. Weak ETag (`W/"abc123"`) means semantically equivalent (e.g. same content, different gzip level). Range requests and If-Match in PUT require strong ETags (RFC 9110 §8.8.1).

Why: strong ETags are the right default for content addressable representations (hash of body). Weak ETags suit cases where the body varies in trivial ways (timestamps, ad insertion) but the cache may still serve from store.

When: derive strong from a content hash of the canonical body. Use weak only when you genuinely need it — most APIs never do.

title: 304 Not Modified responses must not include a message body
content:
RFC 9110 §15.4.5: a 304 response MUST NOT include a body. Headers (ETag, Cache-Control, Vary) MUST match what a 200 would have returned, so caches can update their stored entry's metadata.

Why: clients and proxies short-circuit on 304 and don't attempt to read a body. A non-empty body confuses content-length accounting and may be interpreted as the next response in pipelined connections.

When: every conditional GET. Frameworks usually handle this; verify when writing custom 304 responses by hand.

title: Content-hash ETags over version-counter ETags
content:
Chose content-hash (SHA-256 truncated to 16 hex chars). Alternatives considered: monotonic version counter, updated_at timestamp.

Reason: hash is independent of the storage layer, survives schema migrations, and gives strong semantics (byte-equal bodies share an ETag even across replicas). Counter requires a write on every mutation; timestamp can collide within the same second.

Reversible: yes; clients treat ETags as opaque tokens. Switching the generation strategy invalidates every cached response once, then steady-state resumes.

title: Cursor pagination over offset for large or changing collections
content:
Server returns a `next_cursor` opaque token; client passes it back as `?cursor=<token>`. Inside, the cursor encodes (sort_key, last_id) — a position in the current sort order. Compare offset+limit, which forces a SCAN and skips/duplicates rows on inserts.

Why: offset pagination breaks under concurrent writes. Insert a row at position 5 between two requests, and page 2 either repeats a row or skips one. Cursor pagination is stable across writes and turns the database lookup into a seek instead of a scan.

When: any collection that grows or mutates and any collection larger than ~1000 rows. Tiny static collections can stay on offset.

title: Opaque cursor tokens — clients never parse, never construct
content:
Encode the cursor as a base64url'd JSON or protobuf. Document it as opaque; clients pass back what the server sent and never look inside. The server is free to change the encoding without bumping the API version.

Why: opaque cursors are a server-side implementation detail. The moment a client parses one, the server can no longer migrate the schema (extra sort columns, new tie-breaker) without breaking that client.

When: every cursor scheme. Sign or HMAC the cursor if tampering matters (paginating private data); otherwise plain base64 is fine.

title: Link header for first / next / prev / last navigation
content:
Per RFC 8288, return a `Link` header with `rel="next"`, `rel="prev"`, `rel="first"`, `rel="last"` URIs. Keeps the body free of pagination metadata; works with HEAD; honoured by HTTP clients out of the box.

Why: separating navigation from payload means generic clients (libraries, scripts) can iterate without parsing the body. Body-pagination wrappers (`{data: [], pagination: {...}}`) lock every client into the wrapper format.

When: every paginated endpoint. Some teams emit both — Link header for generic tools, in-body cursor for richer clients. Either way, the Link header is mandatory.

title: Page-number plus page-size on a constantly-changing dataset
content:
Trigger: `?page=3&size=50` on a list endpoint backed by a table that takes constant inserts (events, audit logs, user-generated posts).

Consequence: a row inserted at the head between page 2 and page 3 shifts every subsequent row down by one. The client sees the same row on both pages or skips one entirely. At scale, the database also forces an OFFSET that scans + discards N rows on every page request.

Alternative: cursor pagination (sort_key, last_id) for write-heavy collections. Offset/page-number is fine for small static catalogues — categories, tags, settings — where the cost is negligible and the order is stable.

title: Total-count is expensive — make it opt-in via query param
content:
Computing total row count on a million-row table costs a full scan even with indexes. Default the total to omitted; add `?include=total` (or similar) for callers who actually need the number. Modern UIs increasingly skip totals (`Showing 1-50 of many`).

Why: most clients want the next 50 rows, not the count. Charging every list call for a SELECT COUNT(*) on the hot path scales poorly and is invisible to the consumer. Opt-in keeps the default fast.

When: any large collection. For small collections (< 10k rows) the count is cheap; emit it unconditionally.

title: JWT with alg=none accepted by the verifier
content:
Trigger: library or hand-rolled verifier accepts JWTs whose header declares `"alg": "none"` — meaning unsigned. Some JWT libraries enabled this by default for years.

Consequence: any attacker can forge any token. Strip the signature, set alg to none, the token verifies. Class: complete authentication bypass. Per RFC 8725 §3.1, alg=none must be explicitly disabled.

Alternative: pin the verifier to an allowlist of expected algorithms (`["RS256", "ES256"]`) BEFORE checking the signature. Reject any token whose alg is not in the list, including alg=none. (RFC 8725 §3.1)

title: HS256 verification with the public key as the secret
content:
Trigger: a JWT issuer signs with RS256 (asymmetric, private key signs, public key verifies). The verifier accepts both RS256 and HS256. An attacker mints a token with `"alg": "HS256"` and signs it with the issuer's published public key.

Consequence: HS256 verification uses the same key for sign and verify. The verifier reads the public PEM and uses it as the HMAC secret. The attacker's forged token verifies. Class: complete auth bypass. Known as 'JWT alg confusion' (RFC 8725 §3.1).

Alternative: pin the verifier to one algorithm family per key. Asymmetric keys verify only with their declared asymmetric algorithm. Reject HS256 wholesale if you don't issue HS256 tokens. (RFC 8725 §3.1)

title: JWT verifier without an explicit algorithm allowlist
content:
Trigger: `jwt.verify(token, key)` with no `algorithms: [...]` parameter — the verifier reads the header's `alg` and trusts it.

Consequence: any attacker who can read the token header (always — JWT headers are public) can change alg at will. Combined with alg=none or HS-vs-RS confusion, this is the gateway bug for both classic JWT auth bypasses. (RFC 8725 §3.1 — "Use Appropriate Algorithms")

Alternative: every verify() call passes an explicit `algorithms` allowlist (`["RS256"]` or `["ES256"]`). The header's alg must match; otherwise reject. (RFC 8725 §3.1)

title: JWT aud claim verified against the expected audience
content:
On verify, check the `aud` (audience) claim equals one of the values your service answers for. Reject otherwise (RFC 8725 §3.11). Without an aud check, a token issued for service A is accepted by service B.

Why: tokens leak sideways when services share an issuer but not a trust boundary. The aud claim is the wire-level scope binding. (RFC 8725 §3.11 — "Use Explicit Typing")

When: every JWT verify in every service. Hard-code the expected aud per deployment; fail closed if not present.

title: JWT iss claim verified against an issuer allowlist
content:
Check the `iss` (issuer) claim against a static allowlist before fetching the signing key (RFC 8725 §3.12). Without iss validation, a token from any signer who happens to use a key id (kid) you recognise gets accepted.

Why: the iss claim is the upstream identity boundary. Combined with audience, it pins both ends of the trust chain. (RFC 8725 §3.12)

When: every verify. Maintain the allowlist as configuration, not code; rotate without redeploying.

title: JWT exp, nbf, and iat checked with bounded clock skew
content:
Verify `exp` (expiry, RFC 7519 §4.1.4) is in the future, `nbf` (not-before, §4.1.5) is in the past, and `iat` (issued-at, §4.1.6) is within plausible bounds. Allow ≤5min clock skew on either side. RFC 8725 §3.8 calls out the importance of validating times.

Why: tokens without time validation never expire — a leaked one is a permanent credential. Strict equality breaks under any clock drift; ≤5min skew is the operational sweet spot.

When: every verify. Most JWT libraries default to a 30-60s skew; widen explicitly to 300s.

title: JWT kid header for key rotation without redeploys
content:
Issuer puts the active key's id in the `kid` header. Verifier looks up the public key by kid in a JWKS endpoint or local cache (RFC 8725 §3.7). Rotating keys means publishing the new one and starting to sign with its kid; old tokens verify against the old key until they expire.

Why: without kid, key rotation requires either a hard cutover (every deployed token invalidated) or a multi-key brute-force verify (every token tries every key). kid makes rotation a non-event. (RFC 8725 §3.7)

When: any production JWT issuer. The JWKS endpoint is the public source of truth; cache with a short TTL.

title: Authorization Bearer header is the only safe token carrier
content:
Send tokens in `Authorization: Bearer <token>` (RFC 6750). Never in URL query strings, never in path segments, never in custom headers that aren't logged-aware.

Why: query strings appear in browser history, server access logs, referrer headers on outbound links, and proxy logs. The Authorization header is omitted from logs by every framework's defaults and is excluded from referrer per spec. (RFC 9700 §4.3 — "Access Token Privacy")

When: every authed request. The header is the contract; deviations leak credentials.

title: Access tokens passed in URL query strings or path segments
content:
Trigger: `?access_token=...` or `/api/<token>/users` — sometimes a workaround for browsers that can't set the Authorization header on a redirect or download.

Consequence: the token leaks into browser history, server access logs, CDN edge logs, referrer headers on every outbound link, and any error report that captures URLs. RFC 9700 §4.3.1 explicitly forbids this practice. The leak class is irreversible — once logged, the token is in N systems.

Alternative: Authorization Bearer header for normal requests. For browser-download cases, mint a one-time signed URL with a tight expiry (≤5min) instead of using the long-lived access token. (RFC 9700 §4.3.1)

title: API key in URL path or query string
content:
Trigger: `/api/v1/<api_key>/users` or `?api_key=...` — common in early-stage APIs that haven't wired up header auth yet.

Consequence: same surface as access tokens — server logs, browser history, referrers. Plus API keys are usually long-lived (months or years) and rarely rotated, so a single leak is a permanent breach.

Alternative: `Authorization: Bearer <key>` (RFC 6750) or a custom header with Authorization-equivalent log redaction (`X-API-Key: <key>` works if your logging pipeline strips it). The reasoning mirrors RFC 9700 §4.3.1 for OAuth access tokens — query-string credentials leak through every URL-logging surface. Document key rotation procedure on day one.

title: OAuth 2.0 implicit flow used in 2026
content:
Trigger: SPA or mobile app implements the OAuth 2.0 implicit flow (`response_type=token`) — access token returned directly in the redirect URI fragment.

Consequence: the token is exposed in browser history and may leak via referrer headers. OAuth 2.1 (draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1) removes implicit entirely. RFC 9700 §2.1.2 calls implicit unsafe and requires authorization-code-with-PKCE for all public clients.

Alternative: authorization-code flow with PKCE (RFC 7636) for every client type — SPA, mobile, desktop, server. PKCE removes the need for a client secret and binds the code to the device that requested it. (RFC 9700 §2.1.2)

title: OAuth redirect-URI validation by substring or prefix match
content:
Trigger: server checks the incoming `redirect_uri` with `startswith(allowed_prefix)` or `allowed_uri in incoming_uri`.

Consequence: attackers craft redirect URIs that satisfy the substring check but resolve to an attacker-controlled host — `https://allowed.example.com.evil.tld/cb` passes a prefix check on `https://allowed.example.com`. The authorization code (or token) is sent to the attacker. RFC 9700 §4.1.1 requires exact string match, no wildcards, no prefix.

Alternative: exact string match against a pre-registered list of redirect URIs. Reject anything else. Wildcards and partial matches are explicitly forbidden. (RFC 9700 §4.1.1)

title: OAuth refresh tokens not rotated on every use
content:
Trigger: `/oauth/token` with `grant_type=refresh_token` returns a new access token but the SAME refresh token. Long-lived refresh tokens are convenient.

Consequence: a leaked refresh token is a permanent credential. The legitimate user and the attacker can both refresh independently for as long as the token lives. RFC 9700 §4.13.2 requires rotation: every refresh use issues a new refresh token and invalidates the previous one. Detection: if an old refresh token is reused, both tokens are revoked and the user is forced to re-authenticate.

Alternative: rotate refresh tokens on every use; bind them to a session id; revoke the entire family on reuse-of-old detection. (RFC 9700 §4.13.2)

title: PKCE on every OAuth authorization-code flow
content:
Client generates a random `code_verifier`, sends `code_challenge = SHA-256(code_verifier)` on the /authorize call, and presents the verifier on the /token call (RFC 7636). The server checks the hash matches before issuing tokens.

Why: PKCE binds the authorization code to the device that requested it. An attacker who intercepts the code (via a malicious app, log leak, or browser injection) cannot exchange it without the verifier. OAuth 2.1 §7.4 makes PKCE mandatory for ALL clients (public and confidential).

When: every OAuth flow, including server-side confidential clients. The cost is one hash; the benefit is closing the code-interception attack class. (OAuth 2.1 §7.4 — draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1)

title: Short-lived access tokens with refresh-on-use rotation
content:
Access tokens expire in ≤15 minutes. Refresh tokens last days or weeks but rotate on every use. RFC 9700 §4.13 calls this out as the default modern shape.

Why: short access-token lifetimes bound the blast radius of a leak. Rotation on refresh detects stolen refresh tokens (reuse signals theft, the family is revoked, the user re-authenticates).

When: every OAuth issuer. Some workloads (long-running batch jobs) need longer access tokens; document the exception and rotate the refresh token family aggressively. (RFC 9700 §4.13)

title: OAuth token revocation endpoint per RFC 7009
content:
Expose `POST /oauth/revoke` accepting `token=<value>&token_type_hint=access_token|refresh_token` (RFC 7009). Server invalidates the token immediately; subsequent verifies fail.

Why: logout, account compromise, and credential rotation all need an explicit revoke. Without an endpoint, the only recovery is waiting for natural expiry — minutes for access, weeks for refresh.

When: every OAuth issuer. Pair with a token-introspection endpoint (RFC 7662) for resource servers that need real-time validity checks.

title: Opaque token plus introspection over self-contained JWT
content:
Chose opaque tokens with RFC 7662 introspection. Alternatives considered: signed JWT, encrypted JWT (JWE).

Reason: opaque tokens are revocable instantly (server invalidates the row); JWTs are not (the verifier doesn't talk to the issuer per request). For session-style auth with logout and password-change semantics, instant revocation is the right default. JWT is the right choice when introspection latency matters and tokens are short-lived (≤5 min) so revocation lag is acceptable.

Reversible: yes; flipping the token format is contained in the issuer + a verifier middleware change. Both shapes carry the same claims.

title: JWT typ header pinned to discourage cross-context confusion
content:
Set `typ` in the JWT header to a value specific to the use case (`at+jwt` for access tokens per RFC 9068, `id-token+jwt` for OIDC). Verifier checks typ matches what it expects.

Why: a token issued for context A (e.g. id_token) can otherwise be replayed in context B (e.g. access_token) if the claims happen to overlap. Explicit typing closes the cross-context attack class. (RFC 8725 §3.11)

When: every JWT issuer + verifier. RFC 9068 makes `at+jwt` the standard for OAuth 2.0 access tokens.

title: API mandates that clients store JWTs in browser localStorage
content:
Trigger: API documentation says 'after login, store the JWT in localStorage'. A spec choice on the API side, not a client implementation accident.

Consequence: localStorage is fully accessible to any JavaScript that runs in the page — including XSS payloads. The API has no XSS surface itself, but the prescription forces clients into one. Once XSS happens, every issued JWT is exfiltratable.

Alternative: API issues tokens via httpOnly Secure SameSite cookies, OR ships an SDK that stores tokens in memory + refreshes via a refresh-token cookie. The API contract should never require localStorage; let the client choose the safe path. (RFC 9700 §6 — "Browser-based applications")

title: CORS Allow-Origin wildcard combined with Allow-Credentials true
content:
Trigger: server emits `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` AND `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true` to fix a CORS error during development.

Consequence: browsers refuse the combination per spec — credentialed requests with wildcard origin are blocked. Some servers respond by reflecting Origin into Allow-Origin to bypass, which opens every credentialed endpoint to every origin on the web. With cookies or Authorization headers in scope, any visited page can call your API as the user.

Alternative: explicit origin allowlist. Reflect Origin only when it matches an entry in a static list; otherwise reject. Never wildcard a credentialed endpoint.

title: Reflecting the Origin header into Allow-Origin without an allowlist
content:
Trigger: middleware sets `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: <Origin from request>` whenever Origin is present — 'just echo it back'.

Consequence: every origin on the web is now allowed. Combined with credentials, this is open CORS. Even without credentials, the API leaks data to attacker-controlled pages running JavaScript in the user's browser.

Alternative: maintain an allowlist (env var or config). Reflect Origin only on match; otherwise reject without setting Allow-Origin. The browser will then block the cross-origin response.

title: Explicit CORS origin allowlist — reject the rest
content:
Maintain a list of trusted origins (your web app, partner apps, internal tools). On each request, check `Origin` against the list. Match → set Access-Control-Allow-Origin to the matched value (not `*`). No match → omit Allow-Origin entirely (browser blocks).

Why: an allowlist is the only safe CORS policy for an authed API. The list is small and changes rarely; mismatches fail closed.

When: every authed cross-origin endpoint. Manage the list as configuration, not code, so adding a new tenant doesn't require a deploy.

title: CORS preflight cache via Access-Control-Max-Age
content:
Set `Access-Control-Max-Age: 600` on preflight responses — browsers cache the preflight result for 10 minutes. Saves an OPTIONS round-trip on every subsequent same-method request.

Why: without Max-Age, every non-simple request pays a preflight. For a chatty SPA, that's thousands of redundant OPTIONS calls per session. Browsers cap Max-Age (Chrome at 7200s, Firefox at 86400s); 600s is the safe cross-browser sweet spot.

When: every endpoint that handles preflight. Don't go too high — invalidating a cached preflight requires waiting for the browser cache to expire.

title: Vary: Origin on every CORS response so caches do not poison
content:
When the response's Allow-Origin is computed from the request's Origin header, add `Vary: Origin` so shared caches key on Origin. Without it, a CDN may cache the Allow-Origin value for one origin and serve it to a different origin.

Why: a cached Allow-Origin: https://a.example serving requests from https://b.example silently breaks CORS for B's users. Vary: Origin tells the cache the response varies and prevents the cross-origin cache poisoning.

When: every CORS-enabled response. Pair with the Vary headers for any other request headers used to compute the response (e.g. Authorization).

title: Access-Control-Allow-Headers wildcard reflecting whatever the client asks for
content:
Trigger: server reads `Access-Control-Request-Headers` from preflight and echoes the same set back as Allow-Headers — or sets `Allow-Headers: *`.

Consequence: the API has no idea which headers it actually consumes. Clients send malformed or attacker-controlled headers; logging pipelines record garbage; future audits can't see the real header contract.

Alternative: list the exact headers the API consumes (`Authorization, Content-Type, X-Request-Id`) in Allow-Headers. The list is small and stable; adding a new header is a deliberate change.

title: RateLimit-* response headers signal quota state to clients
content:
Emit `RateLimit-Limit`, `RateLimit-Remaining`, and `RateLimit-Reset` (seconds until reset) on every rate-limited response, not just 429s (draft-ietf-httpapi-ratelimit-headers). Well-behaved clients pre-emptively slow down before they hit the wall.

Why: clients without quota visibility either run flat-out and hit 429s, or apply paranoid back-off that wastes capacity. Headers let them schedule precisely.

When: every rate-limited endpoint. The header names are stabilising in the draft; aliases (`X-RateLimit-*`) are widespread but the unprefixed form is the IETF target.

title: Per-user, per-IP, per-API-key rate-limit buckets in parallel
content:
Maintain three independent buckets for each request: by user (authed), by source IP (anonymous + authed), and by API key (machine clients). Apply the strictest hit. Different abuse vectors fill different buckets.

Why: a single bucket either over-restricts legit users sharing an office IP or under-restricts an attacker rotating user accounts. Layered buckets close both gaps.

When: every public API at scale. The cost is three Redis ops per request; the benefit is precise abuse mitigation.

title: Returning 503 Service Unavailable for rate-limit hits
content:
Trigger: load balancer or app middleware emits 503 when the per-key quota is exceeded — 'we're not serving them right now'.

Consequence: 503 means the system is overloaded; clients (and orchestrators) treat it as a transient infrastructure issue and retry aggressively, often making the situation worse. Monitoring conflates throttling with actual outages.

Alternative: 429 Too Many Requests with `Retry-After` and the RateLimit-* headers. 429 is the throttling-specific signal; clients honour it differently from 503.

title: URL path versioning over header or media-type versioning
content:
Chose `/v1/...`, `/v2/...` in the URL path. Alternatives considered: custom header (`API-Version: 2`), media-type version (`Accept: application/vnd.acme.v2+json`).

Reason: URL versions are cache-friendly (different versions are different URIs), discoverable (curl + browsers reach them), and obvious in logs and dashboards. Header versioning hides the version from access logs, breaks browser-based exploration, and confuses CDNs that cache by URL alone. Media-type versioning is the most RESTful but the least operable.

Reversible: yes; URL → header is a routing change, not a contract change. Most teams that try header versioning end up adding URL versions back for ops visibility.

title: Deprecation and Sunset headers signal lifecycle to clients
content:
Emit `Deprecation: <date>` (draft-ietf-httpapi-deprecation-header) on every response from a deprecated endpoint, and `Sunset: <HTTP-date>` (RFC 8594) for the planned removal date. Clients log warnings and operators can grep for active deprecated callers.

Why: nobody reads release notes. Headers are machine-readable lifecycle signals — clients integrate them into their dashboards and operators see who's still calling deprecated routes.

When: every deprecated endpoint, from the moment deprecation is decided. The headers stay until removal; clients have a moving deadline they can't ignore.

title: Breaking change inside an existing API version
content:
Trigger: removing a field, renaming a parameter, or tightening validation inside `/v1` because 'nobody uses that field' or 'it was always wrong'.

Consequence: silent client breaks. Some caller in some service still depends on the field; their next deploy fails or their request 422s. The breaking change wasn't observable until it shipped, and now rollback is needed.

Alternative: only additive changes inside a version (new fields, new endpoints, new optional params). Breaking changes get a new major version path with a deprecation window on the old one.

title: Additive evolution within a major version — fields, endpoints, optional params
content:
Inside `/v1`, the only safe changes are: add a new optional field, add a new endpoint, accept a new optional query parameter, accept a new value in an open enum. Tightening, removing, or renaming requires `/v2`.

Why: clients written against `/v1` ignore unknown fields and unused endpoints. They break on missing fields, renamed fields, or stricter validation. Additive-only is the contract that lets v1 ship for years.

When: every change to an existing version. Run a 'will old clients still work' check on each PR.

title: Idempotency-Key header for retry-safe POSTs
content:
Client generates a UUID per logical operation and sends it in `Idempotency-Key` (draft-ietf-httpapi-idempotency-key, Stripe convention). Server stores `(key, request_hash, response)` and replays the stored response on retry.

Why: networks drop responses, not requests. Without a key, the client either retries and double-applies, or doesn't retry and loses the operation on a transient failure. The key makes POST as retry-safe as PUT.

When: every state-creating POST a client might retry. Storage TTL ≥24h covers normal client retry windows. Document the header in your API and recommend libraries that auto-set it.

title: Idempotency replay returns the original response, not a fresh one
content:
On a retry with a known Idempotency-Key, the server returns the response it returned the first time — same status, same body, same headers. It does NOT re-execute the operation.

Why: the client may interpret a different response as a new event (e.g. two emails sent for one signup). Replaying the cached response is the contract — same key, same outcome.

When: every key-stored request. Cache the entire response, not just success/failure. Include a header (`Idempotent-Replay: true`) so observability can distinguish first-time from replay traffic.

title: Idempotency-Key TTL surfaced in the response
content:
Document the storage TTL (e.g. 24h) and surface it in the response either as a header (`Idempotency-Expires-At: <date>`) or in the OpenAPI spec. Clients that retry past the TTL get a fresh execution, not a replay.

Why: clients need to know when their idempotency guarantee evaporates. Without the TTL on the wire or in docs, retries past the boundary silently double-apply.

When: every idempotency-supporting endpoint. The TTL is a contract; treat changes to it as breaking.

title: Idempotency-Key reused with a different request body
content:
Trigger: client reuses the same Idempotency-Key for two different operations (different amounts, different recipients).

Consequence: the server has two contradictory choices — replay the first response (silently ignoring the second body) or execute the second (breaking the idempotency contract). Either way, money or state moves wrong.

Alternative: hash the request body alongside the key. On a key match with a different body, return 422 with a problem-details body explaining the conflict. The client must mint a new key for the new operation.

title: Accept header drives content negotiation; default to JSON when ambiguous
content:
Server inspects `Accept` and returns the best supported representation (RFC 9110 §12.5.1). On `*/*` or no Accept, default to `application/json`. On a non-supported Accept, return 406 Not Acceptable.

Why: client-driven negotiation lets one URL serve JSON, CSV, and HTML to different consumers. JSON-default keeps the common case zero-config.

When: any endpoint with multiple representations. For single-format APIs, set Content-Type and skip the negotiation overhead.

title: Content-Type with charset on every text body
content:
Set `Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8` (or the relevant text type with charset). Some clients and middleboxes guess wrong without the charset and corrupt non-ASCII payloads.

Why: charset is part of the media type for text. Browsers and parsers fall back to platform defaults (Latin-1, Windows-1252) when omitted, mangling JSON containing emoji, CJK, or accented Latin.

When: every text response. Binary types (image/*, application/octet-stream) don't take charset.

title: JSON API responding text/html error page on infrastructure failures
content:
Trigger: load balancer or reverse proxy serves an HTML 502/503 page when the backend is down. The page hits a JSON-only client.

Consequence: the client's JSON parser blows up on `<html>...`, the error path raises an unrelated parse exception, and the actual cause (502, backend down) is lost in the parser stack trace. Frontend shows a generic 'unexpected error' instead of 'service unavailable'.

Alternative: configure the LB / proxy to emit JSON error pages with `application/problem+json` for routes prefixed `/api`. Or, inside the app, register a fallback 5xx handler that always returns problem-details regardless of which middleware fires first.

title: 406 Not Acceptable when no representation matches Accept
content:
Per RFC 9110 §15.5.7, when none of the requested media types are supported, return 406 Not Acceptable. The body (any format you choose) lists the types you do support.

Why: 406 is the precise wire signal — `your Accept header didn't match anything I serve`. Returning 200 with whatever you have leaves the client guessing why JSON arrived when they asked for XML.

When: any endpoint with content negotiation. For permissive defaults (JSON-on-anything), skip 406; for strict negotiation, return it.

title: OpenAPI 3.1 as the single source of truth for the API surface
content:
Hand-write or generate `openapi.yaml`; everything else (docs site, client SDKs, validation middleware, mocks) generates from it. Treat the spec as the contract; PR review covers spec changes the same as code changes.

Why: hand-written docs drift from the implementation within weeks. A single spec source means docs, codegen, and tests update together. OpenAPI 3.1 aligns with JSON Schema 2020-12 — the schema fragment in the spec is reusable as a runtime validator.

When: every API. Generated docs (Stoplight, Redoc, Scalar) are the public-facing surface; the spec is the internal artefact.

title: operationId on every endpoint for SDK and client codegen
content:
Set `operationId: createOrder`, `getOrderById`, `listOrders` on every operation in the OpenAPI spec. Generators turn operationId into method names: `client.create_order(...)`. Without it, generators invent names from path + method, often badly.

Why: operationId is the public name of the operation in every generated SDK. Stable, readable names are the difference between an SDK people use and one they don't.

When: every operation, day one. Renaming an operationId is a breaking change for SDK consumers.

title: OpenAPI examples that do not validate against the schema
content:
Trigger: hand-written examples drift from the schema as fields are added or renamed. The example shows `"customerId": "..."`, the schema requires `"customer_id": "..."`.

Consequence: docs misleads readers; codegen with example-driven mock servers serves invalid responses; integration tests written against the docs fail. The bug is invisible until a reader trusts the example.

Alternative: lint examples against the schema in CI. Spectral, Redocly CLI, and openapi-cli all do this. Reject the PR if an example doesn't validate.

title: Error catalogue mirrors the problem-type URIs
content:
Maintain a human-readable docs page listing every problem-type URI: code, title, when raised, what extension fields. Cross-link from the OpenAPI error responses to the catalogue entry.

Why: developers debugging a 422 want to know what `urn:problem-type:account/insufficient-funds` means. A catalogue is the docs surface; the spec is the machine surface. Both stay in sync because both reference the same source.

When: any API with more than ~5 distinct error types. The catalogue is the on-call playbook for support and the integration manual for partners.

title: HEAD support reveals broken framework defaults early
content:
Add a smoke test that issues HEAD on every GET endpoint and asserts the response has matching headers (Content-Length, ETag, Last-Modified) and an empty body. Most frameworks claim HEAD-on-free; many silently mishandle it.

Why: clients and CDNs depend on HEAD parity. The bug usually surfaces only when a real client probes HEAD against an endpoint nobody tested — and the failure is a confusing 'Content-Length mismatch' or empty body where one was expected.

When: any API exposed to the public internet. The test runs in seconds and catches a class of misconfiguration that's otherwise invisible until production.

title: Idempotency-Key conflicts surface real client bugs early
content:
Implement the 'same key, different body → 422' check from day one. The 422s reveal client-side bugs (UUID reuse across operations, mis-scoped key generation) that would otherwise produce silently double-applied operations.

Why: idempotency keys are a contract between server and client. The server's enforcement of the contract is the only signal that the client got it right. Logging the conflict-422 rate is a leading indicator of client correctness.

When: every idempotency-supporting endpoint. The 422 alert threshold is low — a handful per week per client integration is normal during onboarding, then drops to ~zero.
